common_name: Wichita Pecan (Western High-Quality Pecan, Type II Commercial Pecan)

planting.note: Plant bare-root grafted trees in late winter (January–February in zone 9) in deep, well-drained fertile soil; Wichita is a Type II pollinator (pistillate flowers receptive before pollen shed) — plant with a Type I cultivar such as Desirable, Cape Fear, or Western Schley for reliable cross-pollination and maximum yield; Wichita is best adapted to the drier conditions of western Texas, New Mexico, and Arizona where it is one of the two most important commercial varieties.

directions: Plant in deep (4+ feet), well-drained loam or sandy loam in full sun; Wichita is very productive and precocious, bearing significant crops at 5–6 years after planting, earlier than most other varieties; supplement with zinc foliar spray in spring to prevent zinc deficiency; in humid southeastern conditions, Wichita is susceptible to pecan scab and may require fungicide protection. Wichita pecan is one of the most widely planted commercial pecan varieties in the southwestern United States — the dominant variety in the major commercial pecan production areas of western Texas and New Mexico where supplemental irrigation is available and the dry summer climate limits scab disease pressure. The variety was released by the USDA in 1959 and quickly achieved widespread planting in the irrigated orchards of the West Texas Pecan Belt (around San Saba, San Angelo, and the Pecos River region) and New Mexico's Mesilla Valley, where the combination of hot days, cool nights, low humidity, and fertile alluvial soils creates ideal pecan-growing conditions. Wichita produces large to very large nuts (40–45 per pound) with excellent kernel percentage (58–62%) and an exceptional, rich buttery flavor — consistently rated among the highest-quality pecans for flavor by tasting panels. The kernels are plump and well-filled, bright golden-yellow in color, and have the mild, sweet, buttery flavor characteristic of high-quality western-grown pecans. Wichita is a Type II protogynous variety — meaning the pistillate (female) flowers become receptive before the pollen is shed — so it requires pairing with a Type I variety for reliable pollination. Early-bearing, high-quality, and widely available — Wichita is an excellent choice for zone 8–9 home orchard plantings where summer humidity is not extreme.
sources: USDA Agricultural Research Service — Wichita pecan release
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Pecan Variety Selection
New Mexico State University Cooperative Extension — Pecan Production

common_name: Choctaw Pecan (Large-Nut Premium Gulf South Pecan, Type II Late-Season Pecan)

planting.note: Plant bare-root grafted trees in late winter in deep, fertile, well-drained soil; Choctaw is a Type II pollinator — plant alongside Type I cultivars such as Desirable, Cape Fear, or Pawnee for reliable nut set; Choctaw produces some of the largest individual pecan nuts of any variety, rivaled only by Mahan, making it particularly prized for fresh-eating and gift pecans where presentation size matters.

directions: Plant in deep, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; Choctaw is a late-season variety ripening in October–November, later than most other pecans; supplement with zinc spray in spring; Choctaw is somewhat susceptible to pecan scab in very humid coastal conditions and benefits from fungicide applications in wet years; best in zones 7–9 with adequate growing season length. Choctaw pecan is a USDA-ARS variety (released 1959) known primarily for producing some of the largest individual nuts of any commercial pecan variety, with nut counts typically running 35–40 per pound compared to the 50–60 nuts per pound typical of smaller varieties — in pecan marketing, where size commands premium prices, Choctaw's exceptionally large nuts give it distinct commercial value. The nuts are well-filled, with 60–64% kernel percentage, and the kernel quality is excellent — bright golden color, plump halves, and a rich, sweet buttery flavor. Choctaw has broader adaptation through the Gulf South than some varieties, performing reasonably well in the humid coastal plain from eastern Texas through the Florida Panhandle, though like most pecans it requires fungicide management for scab control in high-humidity years. The trees are vigorous and upright-growing, ultimately becoming very large specimens, and are somewhat slow to come into production compared to precocious varieties like Pawnee. The late ripening season (October–November) is an advantage in areas prone to early fall wet weather — the nuts are harvested and on the ground before the worst scab-spreading conditions of mid-fall. A prestige variety for home orchards and direct-market operations where extra-large nut size commands attention.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Pecan Variety Selection

common_name: Caddo Pecan (Scab-Resistant Productive Mid-Season Pecan, Type II Gulf South Pecan)

planting.note: Plant bare-root grafted trees in late winter in deep, fertile, well-drained to moderately moist soil; Caddo is a Type II pollinator — plant with Type I varieties including Desirable, Cape Fear, or Pawnee for maximum nut set; Caddo is particularly recommended for the humid, scab-prone Gulf Coast regions of Louisiana, Mississippi, and coastal Texas where its above-average scab resistance reduces the need for intensive fungicide spray programs.

directions: Plant in deep, fertile soil in full sun; Caddo is consistently high-yielding and somewhat more scab-resistant than varieties like Desirable and Wichita in humid conditions; nuts are medium-sized (50–55 per pound) with excellent filling and flavor; a good choice for home orchards in the humid Gulf South where scab management is a primary concern. Caddo pecan is a USDA-released variety that has become an important recommended variety for the Gulf Coast and southeastern United States, particularly valued for its combination of reliable productivity, above-average scab resistance, and good nut quality in the hot, humid growing conditions of the Gulf South. In the humid pecan-growing regions of Louisiana, Mississippi, and coastal Texas, pecan scab disease (caused by the fungal pathogen Fusicladium effusum) is the primary limiting factor in pecan production — attacking leaves, shoots, and developing nuts in wet spring and summer conditions, reducing kernel quality and yield. Varieties with good scab resistance are substantially more forgiving for home orchardists who cannot maintain the intensive fungicide spray programs used by commercial producers. Caddo's above-average scab tolerance compared to Desirable (which is quite scab-susceptible in humid conditions) makes it a more practical choice for many Gulf South home orchards. The nuts are medium-sized with an attractive appearance, good kernel percentage (57–60%), and solid, sweet pecan flavor. Caddo is also considered a relatively good cross-pollinator as a Type II variety paired with Type I varieties in mixed plantings. Louisiana State University's AgCenter has included Caddo as a recommended variety for Louisiana pecan production for many years.
sources: LSU AgCenter — Pecan Production in Louisiana
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Pecan Variety Selection

common_name: Oconee Pecan (UGA Scab-Resistant Southeastern Pecan, Type I Mid-Season Pecan)

planting.note: Plant bare-root grafted trees in late winter in deep, fertile, well-drained soil; Oconee is a Type I pollinator (sheds pollen before its own pistillate flowers are receptive) — plant with Type II varieties including Stuart, Elliott, Caddo, or Wichita for reliable cross-pollination; Oconee is one of the premier recommendations from University of Georgia Extension for home and commercial pecan production in the southeastern states, specifically selected for performance under southeastern humidity and scab pressure.

directions: Plant in deep, well-drained to moderately moist fertile soil; Oconee is a mid-season variety (ripening October in the Gulf South) with large nuts and good kernel fill; its scab resistance makes it particularly valuable in the humid southeastern states from Virginia through the Gulf Coast; trees are moderately vigorous and productive. Oconee pecan is a University of Georgia-developed variety (released in the 1990s) that has become one of the most highly recommended pecan cultivars for the southeastern United States, specifically bred and selected for performance under the humid conditions, disease pressure, and climate of the southeastern coastal plain and Gulf South. The breeding program at the University of Georgia's Tifton Campus has focused on developing pecan varieties with significantly improved scab resistance compared to the older standard commercial varieties (Desirable, Stuart, Schley) while maintaining nut quality comparable to those benchmarks — and Oconee represents one of the successful outcomes of this program. The nuts are large (40–48 per pound), well-filled, with good kernel percentage (58–62%), and produce a clean, sweet, buttery pecan flavor that compares favorably with the best commercial varieties. Oconee's most important attribute is its considerably better scab resistance than Desirable or Schley in the humid conditions of Georgia, Alabama, and the Gulf South — in UGA variety trials under disease pressure, Oconee consistently shows significantly lower scab infection rates on leaves and shucks, resulting in cleaner nuts and more consistent yields without the intensive fungicide programs that scab-susceptible varieties require. A premier choice for home orchard planting throughout the Gulf South.
sources: University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Pecan Varieties for Georgia
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Southeastern Area

common_name: Cape Fear Pecan (Mid-Atlantic Southeastern Pecan, Type I Reliable Cross-Pollinator)

planting.note: Plant bare-root grafted trees in late winter in deep, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun; Cape Fear is a Type I pollinator (protandrous — sheds pollen before its own pistillate flowers are receptive) and is frequently recommended as a cross-pollinator for Type II varieties including Desirable, Wichita, Pawnee, and Elliott; the Pawnee pecan entry in this database explicitly mentions Cape Fear as one of the recommended Type I companion varieties.

directions: Plant in deep, fertile, well-drained loam in full sun; Cape Fear is a reliable medium-to-large nut producer with moderate scab resistance; it is widely adapted throughout the southeastern states from coastal North Carolina through the Gulf South; trees are vigorous and come into production in 5–7 years; supplement with zinc spray in spring. Cape Fear pecan is a variety developed by the USDA Pecan Breeding Program and named for the Cape Fear River valley of North Carolina, where the variety demonstrated particularly reliable production under the moderately humid coastal plain conditions of the mid-Atlantic South. The variety has proven broadly adapted throughout the southeastern states from eastern North Carolina through the Gulf Coast, performing reliably under a range of humidity levels and soil types typical of the coastal plain and river bottom land of the region. Cape Fear produces medium-to-large nuts (45–52 per pound) with good kernel percentage (57–61%) and solid pecan flavor — not the exceptional nut quality of Desirable or Wichita but consistent, reliable, and commercially acceptable. The variety's primary contribution to many home orchard designs is as the Type I cross-pollinator for the more challenging-to-fruit Type II varieties: since pecan varieties are strongly cross-pollinated (most nut set requires pollen from a different variety), placing Cape Fear alongside Type II varieties like Desirable, Wichita, Elliott, or Stuart ensures reliable pollination and heavier crops on both trees. Cape Fear is also the companion pollinator recommended in many pecan extension publications for home gardeners planting the newer precocious varieties like Pawnee.
sources: USDA Agricultural Research Service — Pecan Breeding
North Carolina Cooperative Extension — Pecan Production
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Pecan Variety Selection
